%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Understand what complex numbers are, how to represent them in rectangular and polar form, and why they are indispensable in signals and systems.

Complex numbers extend the ordinary real number line into a two-dimensional plane. Section B.1 introduces the rectangular form \(z = a + jb\) and the polar form \(z = re^{j\theta}\), and shows how to convert between them. This material is not entirely new — you have likely seen it before — but it deserves a careful review because it appears constantly throughout signals and systems. After this section you will be able to identify the real and imaginary parts of any complex number, compute its magnitude and angle, and switch fluently between rectangular and polar representations. These skills are the foundation for phasors, Fourier analysis, and Laplace transforms.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. B.2](/figures/page-005-fig__b_2-1.png)
*The complex plane (Fig. B.2) anchors the entire section: it shows \(z = a + jb\) as a point with rectangular coordinates \((a, b)\), polar coordinates \((r, \theta)\), and its conjugate \(z^* = a - jb\) reflected across the real axis.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 One complex number, two descriptions: rectangular \(z = a + jb\) and polar \(z = re^{j\theta}\) are the same point viewed differently. The conversion formulas and the most common trap are shown in one reading path.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214363464-1849.png)

## Core Idea

Every complex number \(z\) lives on a two-dimensional plane called the **complex plane**. It has two equivalent descriptions:

$$z = a + jb \quad \text{(rectangular form)}$$

$$z = re^{j\theta} = r(\cos\theta + j\sin\theta) \quad \text{(polar / exponential form)}$$

The **real part** is \(\text{Re}(z) = a\) and the **imaginary part** is \(\text{Im}(z) = b\) — note that \(b\) is a real number, not \(jb\).

The **magnitude** (or modulus) is \(r = |z| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\) and the **angle** (or argument) is \(\theta = \angle z = \arctan(b/a)\).

### MINIMAL EXAMPLE

For \(z = 3 + j4\): magnitude \(r = \sqrt{9+16} = 5\), angle \(\theta = \arctan(4/3) \approx 53.1^\circ\), so \(z = 5e^{j53.1^\circ}\).

### MOST COMMON EXAM TRAP

> The imaginary **part** of \(z = a + jb\) is \(b\), not \(jb\). Confusing the imaginary *term* \(jb\) with the imaginary *part* \(b\) is the single most frequent definition error on exams.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Every complex number \(z = a + jb\) is a point on the 2D complex plane with real part \(a\) and imaginary part \(b\).
- Polar form \(z = re^{j\theta}\) describes the same point using magnitude \(r = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}\) and angle \(\theta\).
- The imaginary part is the real number \(b\), never \(jb\) — a trap that appears on nearly every exam.

*In the next section we will apply complex number arithmetic — addition, multiplication, and conjugation — and see how the polar form turns multiplication into a simple rotation on the complex plane.*
